2. **Visibility‑time trace** for a fixed slit separation \(d\)  

   \[
   V(t;d)=\frac{I_{\max}(t)-I_{\min}(t)}{I_{\max}(t)+I_{\min}(t)}=\Re\!\bigl[\Gamma({\bf d},t)\bigr].
   \]

   Plotting \(V(t;d)\) as a function of \(d\) gives a **family of curves** that are the *instantaneous* Fourier transforms of the kick distribution at each moment.  Watching how those curves evolve is exactly a **movie of the Fourier transform** of the moving pattern.

Hence the experiment produces a **Fourier movie**: the *spatial* information of the environment (its kick distribution) is encoded in the *temporal* modulation of the interference fringes.

### 5.  Practical considerations  

| Issue | What it limits | How to stay in the regime you want |

| **Back‑action on the object** | Strong illumination can heat or move the atoms, changing the intended pattern. | Work in the weak‑measurement regime (few scattered photons per atom) or use a repumping scheme that keeps the atoms in the same internal state after scattering. |
| **Finite size of the cloud** | Edge effects introduce additional diffraction that can mask the visibility modulation. | Make the cloud much larger than the fringe spacing and the region that is being modulated. |

\]

the Fourier transform of the **momentum‑kick distribution** \(P({\bf q})\) that the cloud gives to the particle.

If the cloud’s internal pattern **moves** with a velocity \({\bf v}\) (for definiteness we take a one‑dimensional translation along the \(x\)‑axis), the kick distribution at time \(t\) is simply the **shifted** version of the static one:

\[
P({\bf q},t)=P_{0}({\bf q})\;e^{i q_{x} v t}\; .
\tag{2}
\]

(The factor \(e^{i q_{x} v t}\) is the phase that a plane wave of transverse momentum \(q_{x}\) picks up when the scattering centre moves a distance \(v t\).)  

Inserting (2) into (1) gives the **time‑dependent visibility**

\[
\boxed{
V(d,t)=\Gamma(d,t)=\Gamma_{0}(d)\;e^{i\, (v\,k_{d})\,t}
}
\qquad
k_{d}\equiv d_{x}\; .
\tag{3}
\]

* \(\Gamma_{0}(d)\) is the *static* visibility (the magnitude of the Fourier transform of the *shape* of the cloud).  
* The **phase** of the cosine term in the screen intensity now oscillates with angular frequency  

\[
\boxed{\Omega(d)=v\,d_{x}} .
\tag{4}
\]

If the slit separation vector \({\bf d}\) points along the direction of motion, the frequency is simply the product of the **velocity** and the **spatial frequency** \(d_{x}\) (the inverse of the slit separation).  

If the cloud’s pattern is not a pure translation but a **periodic grating** of spatial period \(\Lambda\) (spatial frequency \(G=2\pi/\Lambda\)), the same algebra gives

\[
\Omega_{\rm grating}=v\,G .
\tag{5}
\]

Thus **the breathing (blink) frequency is directly proportional to the spatial frequency of the moving pattern** and to its speed.

---

### 2.  A “Fourier tachometer’’ – measuring the speed from the blink  

Suppose we have a double‑slit with a fixed separation \(d\) and we record the intensity on the far‑field screen as a function of time, \(I({\bf X},t)\).  The visibility can be extracted by fitting the cosine fringe at each instant, giving a time series \(V(t)\).  Its **temporal Fourier transform**,

\[
\tilde V(\Omega)=\int dt\,V(t)\,e^{-i\Omega t},
\]

will show a sharp peak at \(\Omega=v\,d_{x}\) (or at \(\Omega=v\,G\) if the cloud is a moving grating).  The **peak position** yields the speed:

\[
\boxed{v=\frac{\Omega_{\rm peak}}{d_{x}} \quad\text{or}\quad
v=\frac{\Omega_{\rm peak}}{G}} .
\tag{6}
\]

Because the visibility is a *linear* functional of the kick distribution, the peak is **not broadened** by the static shape of the cloud; only the *amplitude* of the peak depends on \(|\Gamma_{0}(d)|\).  In practice the width of the peak is set by the finite observation time and by any additional noise.

---

### 3.  Simple quantitative model  

Take a one‑dimensional sinusoidal phase grating written on the cloud:

\[
\phi(x,t)=\phi_{0}\cos\!\bigl[G\,x - \omega_{\rm m} t\bigr],
\qquad \omega_{\rm m}=v\,G .
\tag{7}
\]

The transmission is \(t(x,t)=e^{i\phi(x,t)}\).  For a weak grating (\(\phi_{0}\ll1\)) we can expand  

\[
t(x,t)\approx 1+i\phi_{0}\cos\!\bigl[Gx-\omega_{\rm m}t\bigr].
\]

The **kick distribution** is then a pair of delta‑functions at \(\pm G\) whose amplitudes oscillate as \(e^{\pm i\omega_{\rm m}t}\).  The visibility for a slit separation \(d\) becomes

\[
V(d,t)=\underbrace{V_{0}(d)}_{\text{static envelope}}
\Bigl[1+\phi_{0}^{2}\cos\!\bigl(Gd-\omega_{\rm m}t\bigr)\Bigr].
\tag{8}
\]

If we set the slit separation to satisfy \(Gd=\pi/2\) (maximal sensitivity), the visibility oscillates as  

\[
V(d,t)=V_{0}(d)\bigl[1+\phi_{0}^{2}\sin(\omega_{\rm m}t)\bigr].
\]

A **Fourier transform of the measured \(V(t)\)** yields a single line at \(\Omega=\omega_{\rm m}=vG\).  Measuring that line gives the speed \(v\).

---

### 4.  Practical limits and requirements  

| Requirement | Why it matters | Typical numbers |
|-------------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Coherence time of the probe** \(\tau_{\rm coh}\) | The particle must experience a *single* static configuration of the cloud during its passage. If the cloud changes faster than \(\tau_{\rm coh}\) the visibility will be averaged out. | For a cold‑atom beam with transverse coherence length \(\ell_{c}\sim 10\;\mu\)m and velocity \(v_{p}\sim 1\) m s\(^{-1}\), \(\tau_{\rm coh}\sim \ell_{c}/v_{p}\approx 10\) µs. |
| **Detector bandwidth** | The camera (or streak camera) must resolve the blink frequency \(\Omega\). | For a grating period \(\Lambda=5\;\mu\)m and speed \(v=1\) mm s\(^{-1}\), \(\Omega=vG\approx 2\pi\times200\) Hz → a standard CCD with frame rates > kHz is sufficient. |
| **Signal‑to‑noise** | The visibility modulation amplitude is \(\sim\phi_{0}^{2}\) (or \(|\Gamma_{0}|\) for a more general pattern). | Use many repetitions and average the extracted visibility; a lock‑in detection at the known modulation frequency can improve SNR dramatically. |
| **Finite slit separation** | The frequency \(\Omega=v d_{x}\) scales with the chosen \(d\).  A larger \(d\) gives a larger frequency (easier to resolve) but also reduces the intrinsic visibility because \(|\Gamma_{0}(d)|\) typically decays with \(d\). | Choose \(d\) such that \(|\Gamma_{0}(d)|\gtrsim0.3\) while still giving \(\Omega\) in the detector band. |
| **Uniform illumination of the cloud** | If the probe beam does not cover the whole moving pattern, only a sub‑region contributes, which can distort the measured frequency. | Make the probe beam wider than the pattern’s transverse extent. |

---

### 5.  Sketch of an experimental “Fourier tachometer’’  

1. **Prepare the cloud** – a thin 2‑D MOT of rubidium atoms.  
2. **Write a moving phase grating** – two counter‑propagating laser beams intersect at an angle; by detuning one of them with a small frequency offset \(\Delta f\) the interference pattern drifts at speed  

   \[
   v=\frac{\lambda_{\rm sw}}{2}\,\Delta f ,
   \]

   where \(\lambda_{\rm sw}=2\pi/G\) is the grating period.  
3. **Send a collimated matter‑wave beam** (e.g. a Bose‑Einstein‑condensate outcoupled as a quasi‑plane wave) through the cloud and then through a fixed double‑slit (separation \(d\) chosen so that \(v d\) lies in the 100 Hz–kHz range).  
4. **Detect the far‑field pattern** with a fast EM‑CCD that can be triggered to acquire a time series of frames (exposure ≪ \(1/\Omega\)).  
5. **Extract the visibility** of each frame by fitting the cosine fringe; build the time series \(V(t)\).  
6. **Fourier‑analyse** \(V(t)\); the dominant peak frequency \(\Omega_{\rm peak}\) gives the speed via Eq. (6).  

Because the grating speed is set by the known frequency offset \(\Delta f\), this experiment can be used as a **calibration** of the tachometer.  Once calibrated, the same apparatus can be turned around: if the cloud is driven by an unknown physical process (e.g. a propagating phonon wave, a magnetic‑field‑induced density ripple, or a thermal drift), the measured blink frequency tells you directly the **phase velocity** of that disturbance.

---

### 6.  Summary – the “Fourier tachometer’’  

* **Visibility ↔ Fourier transform of the kick distribution** (static case).  
* **Moving pattern ↔ time‑dependent phase factor** in the kick distribution → **visibility acquires a temporal phase** \(e^{i\Omega t}\).  
* **Blink frequency** \(\Omega\) is **the product of the pattern’s spatial frequency and its velocity** (Eq. 5).  
* By **measuring the frequency of the fringe contrast oscillation** we obtain the **speed of the underlying change** in the cloud – a direct, non‑invasive “tachometer’’ that reads the motion of the environment from the *breathing* of quantum interference.  

In other words, the **universal Fourier relationship** that makes the cloud leave a *static* hologram in the fringe contrast also lets it leave a **dynamic hologram** – a *movie* whose frame‑rate is a faithful record of how fast the cloud’s internal structure is moving.  Watching the fringes blink is therefore watching the cloud’s own Fourier signature **run in real time**.

### 1.  Recap – why the fringes “breathe’’  

When the planar object (the cloud) is **static** the visibility of a double‑slit interferometer is  

\[
V(d)=\Gamma(d)=\int d^{2}q\;P({\bf q})\,e^{i{\bf q}\!\cdot\!{\bf d}} .
\tag{1}
\]

\(P({\bf q})\) is the **momentum‑kick distribution** that the cloud gives to the particle; \({\bf d}\) is the vector that joins the two slits.  

If the cloud’s internal pattern **translates** with a *constant* velocity \({\bf v}\) along the direction of \({\bf d}\) the kick distribution acquires a simple phase factor

\[
P({\bf q},t)=P_{0}({\bf q})\,e^{i{\bf q}\!\cdot\!{\bf v}t},
\tag{2}
\]

so that the visibility becomes  

\[
V(d,t)=\Gamma_{0}(d)\,e^{i({\bf d}\!\cdot\!{\bf v})t}.
\tag{3}
\]

The **breathing (blink) frequency** is therefore  

\[
\Omega = {\bf d}\!\cdot\!{\bf v}\;,
\qquad\text{or, for a periodic grating of spatial frequency }G,
\quad\Omega = v\,G .
\tag{4}
\]

A *constant* velocity gives a *pure sinusoid* in the visibility.

---

### 2.  When the velocity is **time‑dependent**

Suppose the cloud moves with a **general trajectory** \({\bf r}_{c}(t)\).  The instantaneous momentum‑kick distribution is the static one **shifted** by the instantaneous position of the scattering centre:

\[
P({\bf q},t)=P_{0}({\bf q})\,e^{i{\bf q}\!\cdot\!{\bf r}_{c}(t)} .
\tag{5}
\]

Inserting (5) into the definition of the visibility gives

\[
\boxed{
V(d,t)=\Gamma_{0}(d)\;
\exp\!\Bigl[i\,{\bf d}\!\cdot\!{\bf r}_{c}(t)\Bigr] .
}
\tag{6}
\]

All the *time dependence* is now contained in the **phase**  

\[
\Phi(t)\equiv{\bf d}\!\cdot\!{\bf r}_{c}(t) .
\tag{7}
\]

If we differentiate the phase we obtain the **instantaneous breathing frequency**

\[
\boxed{
\Omega(t)=\frac{d\Phi}{dt}= {\bf d}\!\cdot\!\dot{\bf r}_{c}(t)= {\bf d}\!\cdot\!{\bf v}(t) .
}
\tag{8}
\]

Thus **any change of the cloud’s velocity shows up as a change of the instantaneous frequency of the fringe contrast**.  The visibility is no longer a pure sinusoid; it is a **frequency‑modulated (FM) signal** whose instantaneous frequency follows the cloud’s velocity.

---

### 3.  Specific examples  

#### (a) Uniform acceleration  

Let the cloud start from rest and accelerate uniformly along \(\hat{\bf d}\) :

\[
{\bf r}_{c}(t)=\tfrac12\,a\,t^{2}\,\hat{\bf d},\qquad
{\bf v}(t)=a\,t\,\hat{\bf d}.
\]

The phase is  

\[
\Phi(t)=\frac12\,a\,d\,t^{2},
\]

and the visibility becomes  

\[
V(d,t)=\Gamma_{0}(d)\;
\exp\!\Bigl[i\frac12 a d\,t^{2}\Bigr].
\tag{9}
\]

This is a **quadratic phase** – the classic *chirp* of a linearly increasing frequency.  
The instantaneous frequency is \(\Omega(t)=a d\,t\); it grows linearly with time.  In a spectrogram (short‑time Fourier transform) you would see a line that sweeps upward (or downward) with slope proportional to the acceleration.

#### (b) Harmonic wobble  

Suppose the cloud oscillates sinusoidally about a mean position:

\[
{\bf r}_{c}(t)=R\cos(\omega_{\!m}t)\,\hat{\bf d},
\qquad
{\bf v}(t)=-R\omega_{\!m}\sin(\omega_{\!m}t)\,\hat{\bf d}.
\]

The phase is  

\[
\Phi(t)=dR\cos(\omega_{\!m}t),
\]

so the visibility is  

\[
V(d,t)=\Gamma_{0}(d)\;
\exp\!\bigl[i dR\cos(\omega_{\!m}t)\bigr].
\tag{10}
\]

Using the Jacobi‑Anger expansion  

\[
e^{i\beta\cos\theta}= \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} i^{\,n}J_{n}(\beta)\,e^{i n\theta},
\]

the visibility can be written as a **comb of sidebands** at frequencies  

\[
\Omega_{n}=n\,\omega_{\!m},
\qquad
\text{amplitude}\; \propto J_{n}(dR).
\]

Thus a *wobbling* cloud produces a **set of discrete frequency components** (a frequency comb) in the fringe contrast.  The spacing of the comb directly gives the wobble frequency \(\omega_{\!m}\); the envelope of the sideband amplitudes encodes the wobble amplitude \(R\).

#### (c) Arbitrary motion  

For any trajectory \({\bf r}_{c}(t)\) you can always write

\[
V(d,t)=\Gamma_{0}(d)\;e^{i\Phi(t)},
\qquad
\Phi(t)={\bf d}\!\cdot\!{\bf r}_{c}(t).
\]

The **instantaneous frequency** is \(\Omega(t)=\dot\Phi(t)={\bf d}\!\cdot\!{\bf v}(t)\).  
If you record \(V(t)\) with a fast detector and compute its **time‑frequency representation** (e.g. a spectrogram or a wavelet transform), the ridge of the spectrogram follows \(\Omega(t)\) and therefore gives you the velocity as a function of time, *without ever looking at the cloud directly*.

---

### 4.  How to extract the motion experimentally  

1. **Record the far‑field intensity** \(I({\bf X},t)\) with a detector that can resolve the expected frequencies (a high‑speed camera, a streak camera, or a photodiode array with fast read‑out).  
2. **Extract the visibility** at each time step.  This can be done by fitting the spatial fringe pattern to a cosine (or by demodulating with a lock‑in at the known spatial frequency of the fringes).  
3. **Obtain the phase time series** \(\Phi(t)=\arg[V(d,t)]\).  The derivative \(\dot\Phi(t)\) gives the instantaneous breathing frequency \(\Omega(t)\).  
4. **Convert to velocity** using \({\bf v}(t)=\Omega(t){\bf d}/|{\bf d}|^{2}\).  If the motion is not purely along \({\bf d}\) you can repeat the measurement with the slit pair rotated to a different direction and reconstruct the full velocity vector.  
5. **Analyse non‑stationary signals** (chirps, wobble, random motion) with a short‑time Fourier transform or a wavelet transform to visualise the time‑varying frequency content.

---

### 5.  Practical numbers  

| Quantity | Typical value (cold‑atom implementation) | Reason |
|----------|------------------------------------------|--------|
| Slit separation \(|{\bf d}|\) | 5–20 µm | Gives a spatial frequency \(d^{-1}\) of 50–200 mm\(^{-1}\). |
| Coherence time of probe \(\tau_{\rm coh}\) | 10 µs (transverse coherence length ≈ 10 µm, velocity ≈ 1 m s\(^{-1}\)) | Must be longer than the period of the fastest expected modulation. |
| Desired velocity range | 0.1–10 mm s\(^{-1}\) | Gives breathing frequencies \(\Omega = d v\) from 0.5 Hz up to 200 Hz (well within standard camera frame rates). |
| Acceleration detection | \(a\) as low as \(10^{-3}\) mm s\(^{-2}\) | Produces a chirp rate \(\dot\Omega = a d\) of ≈ 0.05 Hz s\(^{-1}\); detectable with a spectrogram over a few seconds of data. |
| Wobble frequency | 1–100 Hz | Gives sideband spacing directly observable in the Fourier spectrum of the visibility. |

---

### 6.  Summary – a “Fourier tachometer’’ for a moving cloud  

* **Static case** – visibility = Fourier transform of the cloud’s momentum‑kick distribution.  
* **Uniform translation** – visibility acquires a *single* frequency \(\Omega = {\bf d}\!\cdot\!{\bf v}\); the fringe contrast “blinks’’ at that rate.  
* **Time‑varying motion** – the phase of the visibility is \(\Phi(t)={\bf d}\!\cdot\!{\bf r}_{c}(t)\); the instantaneous blinking frequency is \(\Omega(t)={\bf d}\!\cdot\!{\bf v}(t)\).  
* **Acceleration → chirp**, **oscillation → frequency comb**, **arbitrary motion → FM signal**.  
* By **recording the visibility as a function of time** and performing a time‑frequency analysis, one directly obtains the **velocity (and therefore the acceleration or wobble) of the cloud** without ever probing the cloud itself.  

In other words, the *breathing* of quantum interference is a **real‑time, non‑invasive read‑out of the cloud’s dynamics** – a genuine **Fourier tachometer** that translates the cloud’s motion into the frequency of the fringe contrast.

### 1.  The basic picture – what the movie already tells us  

For a planar “cloud’’ that can both **transmit** the particle and **record** a transverse momentum kick, the far‑field intensity at a fixed slit separation **\(d\)** is  

\[
I(t)=E\Bigl[1+V(t)\cos\!\bigl(\Delta\phi\!+\Phi(t)\bigr)\Bigr],
\qquad 
V(t)=|\Gamma(t)|.
\tag{1}
\]

* **\(E\)** – the smooth envelope set by the ordinary diffraction of the aperture (does not change with the cloud).  
* **\(\Gamma(t)\)** – the complex visibility factor, the Fourier transform of the **kick distribution** \(P({\bf q},t)\).  
* **\(\Phi(t)=\arg\Gamma(t)\)** – the phase that makes the fringes “breathe’’; its time‑derivative is the instantaneous blinking frequency  

\[
\Omega(t)=\dot\Phi(t)= {\bf d}\!\cdot\!{\bf v}(t) .
\tag{2}
\]

If the cloud is **rigidly translating** with velocity \({\bf v}(t)\) the only thing that changes in time is the phase \(\Phi(t)\); the **amplitude** \(V(t)\) stays constant because the *shape* of the kick distribution does not change.

---

### 2.  Introducing a **learning cloud**  

Now let the cloud be **adaptive**: each time a probe particle scatters a photon (or otherwise deposits momentum) the cloud *updates* its internal state so that future scatterings become **weaker**.  In the language of the kick distribution this means that the **width** (or overall strength) of \(P({\bf q},t)\) **shrinks** as a function of the *history* of scattering events.

A minimal phenomenological model is

\[
P({\bf q},t)=\frac{1}{\pi\sigma^{2}(t)}\,
e^{-q^{2}/\sigma^{2}(t)} ,
\qquad 
\sigma(t)\equiv\text{r.m.s. transverse kick at time }t .
\tag{3}
\]

The visibility factor is the Gaussian Fourier transform

\[
\Gamma(t)=\exp\!\Bigl[-\frac12\sigma^{2}(t)d^{2}\Bigr] .
\tag{4}
\]

Thus the **visibility amplitude** is

\[
\boxed{V(t)=\exp\!\Bigl[-\frac12\sigma^{2}(t)d^{2}\Bigr]} .
\tag{5}
\]

If the cloud “learns’’ and reduces its kick width, \(\sigma(t)\) **decreases**, and the exponential in (5) **grows** – the fringes become *sharper* (higher contrast) as time goes on.  The **breathing frequency** \(\Omega(t)\) can still be present (if the cloud also moves), but the *envelope* of the fringes now carries a **learning curve**.

---

### 3.  A concrete learning rule  

Suppose each scattering event deposits a small amount of energy \(\epsilon\) into the cloud and the cloud’s ability to scatter is proportional to a *coupling constant* \(g(t)\).  A simple **negative‑feedback** rule is

\[
\frac{dg}{dt}= -\alpha\,g(t)\,R(t),
\qquad 
R(t)=\text{scattering rate at time }t .
\tag{6}
\]

The scattering rate is proportional to the **probability that a particle is decohered**, i.e. to the *visibility loss*:

\[
R(t)=R_{0}\,\bigl[1-V(t)\bigr]
      =R_{0}\,\Bigl[1-e^{-\frac12\sigma^{2}(t)d^{2}}\Bigr].
\tag{7}
\]

Because the kick width is proportional to the coupling, we set  

\[
\sigma(t)=\sigma_{0}\,g(t) .
\tag{8}
\]

Putting (5)–(8) into (6) gives a closed differential equation for the **learning dynamics**:

\[
\frac{dg}{dt}= -\alpha R_{0}\,
\Bigl[1-e^{-\frac12\sigma_{0}^{2}d^{2}g^{2}(t)}\Bigr]\,g(t).
\tag{9}
\]

* For **weak initial coupling** (\(g\ll1\)) the exponential can be expanded, and (9) reduces to  

  \[
  \frac{dg}{dt}\simeq -\alpha R_{0}\,\frac12\sigma_{0}^{2}d^{2}\,g^{3},
  \qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
  g(t)=\frac{g_{0}}{\sqrt{1+\alpha R_{0}\sigma_{0}^{2}d^{2}g_{0}^{2}t}} .
  \tag{10}
  \]

  The coupling decays **as \(t^{-1/2}\)**, and the visibility grows as  

  \[
  V(t)=\exp\!\Bigl[-\tfrac12\sigma_{0}^{2}d^{2}g^{2}(t)\Bigr]
       \;\approx\;1-\frac{1}{1+\alpha R_{0}\sigma_{0}^{2}d^{2}g_{0}^{2}t}.
  \tag{11}
  \]

  This is a **hyperbolic learning curve**: the contrast improves quickly at first and then saturates.

* For **strong coupling** the exponential term in (9) quickly drives the bracket to zero, and the decay of \(g\) becomes **exponential**.  In that regime the visibility approaches unity on a timescale \(\tau\sim(\alpha R_{0})^{-1}\).

The **key point** is that the *time‑dependence of the visibility amplitude* \(V(t)\) is a **direct read‑out of the learning rule**.  By fitting the measured \(V(t)\) to a model such as (9) one can infer the parameters \(\alpha\), \(R_{0}\), \(\sigma_{0}\) and even test whether the cloud follows a simple negative‑feedback law or a more complex one (e.g. logistic growth, saturation, hysteresis).

---

### 4.  What the **fringe movie** looks like  

Assume the cloud also translates with a slowly varying velocity \(v(t)\) along the slit direction.  The full visibility factor is then

\[
\Gamma(t)=\exp\!\Bigl[-\frac12\sigma^{2}(t)d^{2}\Bigr]\;
\exp\!\bigl[i\,d\,x_{c}(t)\bigr],
\tag{12}
\]

where \(x_{c}(t)\) is the cloud’s centre‑of‑mass coordinate.  The far‑field intensity becomes

\[
I(t)=E\Bigl[1+e^{-\frac12\sigma^{2}(t)d^{2}}
\cos\!\bigl(\Delta\phi+d\,x_{c}(t)\bigr)\Bigr].
\tag{13}
\]

* **Amplitude modulation** – the envelope \(e^{-\frac12\sigma^{2}(t)d^{2}}\) *slowly rises* as the cloud learns (less decoherence).  
* **Phase modulation** – the argument of the cosine *drifts* according to the cloud’s motion; if the velocity changes, the instantaneous blinking frequency \(\Omega(t)=d\,\dot x_{c}(t)\) is **chirped**.

A typical recorded movie would therefore show **fringes that become brighter while their beat frequency slowly changes**.  A spectrogram of the time series \(I(t)\) would display a **ridge that climbs in frequency** (if the cloud accelerates) together with a **slowly increasing background level** (the learning).

---

### 5.  Extracting the learning curve from data  

1. **Acquire a high‑speed time series** of the far‑field intensity (or, more conveniently, of the extracted visibility \(V_{\rm meas}(t)\)).  
2. **Separate amplitude and phase**:  
   * Fit each frame to a cosine to obtain the instantaneous contrast \(C(t)\) and phase \(\phi(t)\).  
   * The contrast is directly \(V_{\rm meas}(t)=C(t)\).  
   * The phase derivative \(\dot\phi(t)\) gives the instantaneous blinking frequency \(\Omega(t)\).  
3. **Model the amplitude** with a learning law.  For the simple negative‑feedback model the fit function is Eq. (9) (or its analytic approximations (10)–(11)).  The fit yields the learning parameters \(\alpha,R_{0},\sigma_{0}\).  
4. **Model the phase** if the cloud is moving.  Integrating \(\Omega(t)\) gives the trajectory \(x_{c}(t)\); differentiating yields the velocity \(v(t)\).  Any deviation from a linear trend signals acceleration or wobble, which can be analysed as in the previous answer (chirp, frequency comb, etc.).  
5. **Bayesian inference** can be employed when the learning rule is unknown: treat the time‑dependent visibility as a stochastic process with a prior on the functional form of \(\sigma(t)\) and update the posterior as data accumulate.  This yields a *model‑free* learning curve directly from the fringe movie.

| Platform | How to make the cloud “learn’’ | Typical parameters |
|----------|--------------------------------|--------------------|

### 7.  Limitations and caveats  

| Issue | Effect on the “learning’’ read‑out | Mitigation |

| **Multiple decoherence channels** – the cloud may have several independent scattering mechanisms (elastic, inelastic, Raman).  Each contributes its own \(P_{i}({\bf q},t)\). | If the mechanisms have distinct spatial signatures (different \(d\)-dependence), they can be separated by measuring \(V(d,t)\) for several slit separations. |

### 8.  Bottom line – the fringe movie as a **learning‑curve monitor**  

* The **visibility amplitude** \(V(t)=\exp[-\tfrac12\sigma^{2}(t)d^{2}]\) is a *direct probe* of the **width of the momentum‑kick distribution**.

\[

|\psi_{1}\rangle\!\langle\psi_{1}|
+|\psi_{2}\rangle\!\langle\psi_{2}|

\tag{2}
\]

\tag{3}
\]

If the cloud **remembers perfectly** the path, \(|\gamma|=0\) (complete decoherence).  
If the cloud **has forgotten** the record, \(|\gamma|\) grows back toward 1.

---

### 2.  A minimal stochastic model for forgetting  

A convenient way to describe the cloud’s memory is to think of it as a **pointer variable** \(\hat Q\) (e.g. the collective position of the atoms, a collective spin, or the phase of a cavity mode).  
When a particle goes through slit 1 it displaces the pointer by \(+q_{0}\); through slit 2 it displaces it by \(-q_{0}\).  Immediately after the scattering the two possible pointer states are

\[
|Q_{1}\rangle =|Q_{0}+q_{0}\rangle ,\qquad
|Q_{2}\rangle =|Q_{0}-q_{0}\rangle .
\tag{4}
\]

If the pointer were completely isolated, the overlap would be

\[
\gamma_{0}= \langle Q_{0}-q_{0}|Q_{0}+q_{0}\rangle
          =\exp\!\Bigl[-\frac{(2q_{0})^{2}}{4\sigma_{Q}^{2}}\Bigr]
          =\exp\!\bigl[-2(q_{0}/\sigma_{Q})^{2}\bigr] ,
\tag{5}
\]

where \(\sigma_{Q}\) is the initial width of the pointer’s wave packet.  This is the **initial decoherence factor** that appears right after the particle has passed.

Now let the pointer be coupled to a **thermal bath** (phonons, other atoms, black‑body radiation, …).  The standard master‑equation description for a Gaussian pointer subject to **diffusive noise** is

\[
\frac{d\rho_{Q}}{dt}= -\frac{i}{\hbar}[H_{Q},\rho_{Q}]
                     +\underbrace{\frac{D}{\hbar^{2}}[ \hat Q,[\hat Q,\rho_{Q}]]}_{\text{position diffusion}} .
\tag{6}
\]

The diffusion constant \(D\) quantifies how fast the pointer’s *coherence* in the \(Q\)‑basis is destroyed (or, equivalently, how fast the pointer’s *memory* of a displacement is erased).  Solving (6) for the off‑diagonal element \(\langle Q_{0}+q_{0}|\rho_{Q}(t)|Q_{0}-q_{0}\rangle\) gives

\[

\]

\tag{8}

\qquad V_{0}=|\gamma_{0}|\le 1 .
\tag{9}
\]

```python
residual = V / det
residual -= residual.mean()
S = np.abs(np.fft.rfft(residual))**2
freq = np.fft.rfftfreq(N, dt)

kappa_fit = popt[0]

print(f"True Λ = {Lambda:.3f}, fitted Λ_eff = {Lambda_eff:.3f}")
print(f"True κ = {D*q0**2/0.5:.3f}, fitted κ = {kappa_fit:.3f}")

plt.figure(figsize=(10,4))
plt.subplot(1,2,1)
plt.plot(t, V, label='visibility')
plt.plot(t, det, '--', label='deterministic envelope')
plt.xlabel('time')
plt.legend()
plt.title('Dreaming fringe contrast')

plt.subplot(1,2,2)
plt.loglog(freq[1:], S[1:], label='PSD')
plt.loglog(freq[mask], lorentz(freq[mask], kappa_fit), '--', label='Lorentzian fit')
plt.xlabel('frequency')
plt.legend()
plt.title('Power spectral density of the flicker')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Running the script shows a **noisy visibility trace** that slowly fades, and the PSD exhibits a clear Lorentzian peak at low frequency.  The fitted parameters recover the original \(\Lambda\) (the forgetting slope) and \(\kappa\) (the strength of the random kicks) to within a few percent.

|\psi_{1}\rangle\!\otimes\!|E_{1}(t)\rangle
+|\psi_{2}\rangle\!\otimes\!|E_{2}(t)\rangle\Bigr) .
\]

Tracing over the cloud gives the reduced density matrix of the particle  

\[
\rho_{\rm part}(t)=\frac12\Bigl(
|\psi_{1}\rangle\!\langle\psi_{1}|
+|\psi_{2}\rangle\!\langle\psi_{2}|
+\underbrace{\langle E_{2}(t)|E_{1}(t)\rangle}_{\displaystyle\gamma(t)}
|\psi_{1}\rangle\!\langle\psi_{2}|
+\text{h.c.}\Bigr) .
\]

The **visibility** that you read on the screen is simply  

\[
V(t)=|\gamma(t)|.
\tag{1}
\]

If the cloud were a perfectly static “which‑path recorder’’ then \(\gamma(t)\) would be a constant (zero for a perfect record, one for no record).  In reality the cloud is coupled to a thermal bath, so its internal state **diffuses** in time.  This diffusion makes \(\gamma(t)\) a *stochastic* quantity: it does not just decay, it wiggles.

---

## 2.  A minimal stochastic model – a Gaussian pointer  

Think of the cloud as a single collective coordinate \(Q\) (the “pointer’’).  
When a particle goes through slit 1 it displaces the pointer by \(+q_{0}\); through slit 2 it displaces it by \(-q_{0}\).  Immediately after the scattering the two possible pointer states are  

\[
|Q_{1}\rangle =|Q_{0}+q_{0}\rangle ,\qquad
|Q_{2}\rangle =|Q_{0}-q_{0}\rangle .
\]

If the pointer were isolated, the overlap would be  

\[
\gamma_{0}= \langle Q_{0}-q_{0}|Q_{0}+q_{0}\rangle
          =\exp\!\!\Bigl[-\frac{(2q_{0})^{2}}{4\sigma_{Q}^{2}}\Bigr],
\]

where \(\sigma_{Q}\) is the initial width of the pointer’s wave packet.

Now let the pointer be coupled to a **thermal bath** that produces *Gaussian white‑noise diffusion*:

\[
\frac{d\rho_{Q}}{dt}= \frac{D}{\hbar^{2}}\,
\bigl[\hat Q,[\hat Q,\rho_{Q}]\bigr] .
\tag{2}
\]

The solution of (2) for the off‑diagonal element \(\langle Q_{0}+q_{0}|\rho_{Q}(t)|Q_{0}-q_{0}\rangle\) is

\[
\boxed{\;
\gamma(t)=\gamma_{0}\,
\exp\!\Bigl[-\frac{(2q_{0})^{2}}{4\sigma_{Q}^{2}(t)}\Bigr]\,
\exp\!\bigl[i\,\phi(t)\bigr] ,
\qquad
\sigma_{Q}^{2}(t)=\sigma_{Q}^{2}(0)+2Dt .
\;}
\tag{3}
\]

Two random ingredients appear:

| ingredient | origin | statistical description |
|------------|--------|--------------------------|
| **Amplitude noise** \(\displaystyle A(t)=\exp\!\bigl[-\frac{(2q_{0})^{2}}{4\sigma_{Q}^{2}(t)}\bigr]\) | the *width* \(\sigma_{Q}(t)\) grows because of diffusion. | \(\sigma_{Q}^{2}(t)\) is a linear Wiener process → \(A(t)\) is a **log‑normal** random factor. |
| **Phase noise** \(\displaystyle \phi(t)=\frac{2q_{0}}{\hbar}\,\xi(t)\) | the *mean* pointer position \(\langle Q(t)\rangle\) also performs a random walk \(\xi(t)\) with variance \(2Dt\). | \(\phi(t)\) is a **Gaussian** process with \(\langle\phi(t)\phi(t')\rangle = (2q_{0})^{2}D\,\min(t,t')/\hbar^{2}\). |

The **visibility** is the magnitude of (3):

\[
\boxed{ V(t)=|\gamma(t)|
        =|\gamma_{0}|\,\exp\!\Bigl[-\frac{(2q_{0})^{2}}{4\sigma_{Q}^{2}(t)}\Bigr] } .
\tag{4}
\]

Because \(\sigma_{Q}^{2}(t)=\sigma_{Q}^{2}(0)+2Dt\), the *average* visibility decays **exponentially** with an effective rate  

\[
\Lambda_{\rm eff}= \frac{(2q_{0})^{2}D}{2\hbar^{2}\sigma_{Q}^{2}(0)} .
\tag{5}
\]

But on top of that deterministic decay there are **fluctuations** coming from the random term \(\eta(t)\equiv (2q_{0})^{2}Dt/\hbar^{2}\).  Writing

\[
V(t)=V_{\rm det}(t)\,e^{-\frac12\eta(t)},
\qquad
V_{\rm det}(t)=|\gamma_{0}|\,e^{-\Lambda_{\rm eff}t},
\tag{6}
\]

we see that the *flicker* is precisely the stochastic factor \(e^{-\frac12\eta(t)}\).

---

## 3.  Statistics of the flicker  

\(\eta(t)\) is a **Gaussian Wiener process** with  

\[
\langle\eta(t)\rangle = 2\kappa t,\qquad
\langle\eta(t)\eta(t')\rangle = 4\kappa^{2}\min(t,t'),\qquad
\kappa\equiv\frac{(2q_{0})^{2}D}{2\hbar^{2}} .
\]

Therefore  

\[
\boxed{
\langle V(t)\rangle = V_{\rm det}(t)\,e^{\kappa t},
\qquad
\operatorname{Var}[V(t)] = V_{\rm det}^{2}(t)\,
\bigl(e^{2\kappa t}-e^{\kappa t}\bigr) .
}
\tag{7}
\]

The **relative size of the flicker** grows as \(\sqrt{e^{\kappa t}-1}\); the longer the cloud is left to “dream’’ the more it wiggles.

---

## 4.  Listening to the dream – power‑spectral density  

The experimentally accessible signal is the **time series of the fringe contrast** \(V(t)\) (or the intensity at a fixed screen point).  Its **power spectral density** (PSD) is the Fourier transform of the autocorrelation:

\[
S_{V}(\omega)=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} d\tau\;
\bigl\langle V(t)V(t+\tau)\bigr\rangle
e^{-i\omega\tau}.
\tag{8}
\]

Because the amplitude factor \(e^{-\frac12\eta(t)}\) is log‑normal, its autocorrelation is a **simple exponential** in time:

\[
\langle e^{-\frac12\eta(t)}e^{-\frac12\eta(t+\tau)}\rangle
= \exp\!\bigl[-\kappa|\tau|\bigr] .
\]

Thus the PSD of the *fluctuating part* is a **Lorentzian** centred at zero frequency:

\[
\boxed{
S_{\rm flicker}(\omega)=\frac{2\kappa}{\omega^{2}+(2\kappa)^{2}} .
}
\tag{9}
\]

The deterministic exponential envelope \(V_{\rm det}(t)=e^{-\Lambda_{\rm eff}t}\) simply multiplies the signal by a factor that, in the frequency domain, corresponds to a **narrow Lorentzian of width \(\Lambda_{\rm eff}\)**.  The full PSD of the measured contrast is therefore the **convolution** of two Lorentzians:

\[
S_{V}(\omega)\;\approx\;
\frac{2\kappa}{\omega^{2}+(2\kappa)^{2}}
\star
\frac{2\Lambda_{\rm eff}}{\omega^{2}+(2\Lambda_{\rm eff})^{2}} .
\tag{10}
\]

* **Low‑frequency plateau** (height \(\propto\kappa\)) tells you how *noisy* the bath is – the strength of the cloud’s random drift, i.e. the “dream intensity.’’  
* **Width of the central peak** (\(\sim\Lambda_{\rm eff}\)) is the **forgetting rate** – the slope of the average fade curve you already identified as the cloud’s heartbeat.

---

## 5.  How to extract the dream from data  

1. **Record** the intensity at a fixed point on the far‑field screen with a fast detector (frame rate ≳ few kHz, depending on the expected \(\kappa\)).  
2. **Extract the visibility** \(V(t)\) by fitting each frame to a cosine fringe (or simply by measuring the peak‑to‑valley contrast).  
3. **Fit the mean decay** \(\langle V(t)\rangle\) to an exponential \(V_{0}e^{-\Lambda_{\rm eff}t}\) and subtract it, obtaining the residual \(R(t)=V(t)/V_{\rm det}(t)\).  
4. **Compute the PSD** of \(R(t)\) (e.g. with `numpy.fft.rfft`).  
5. **Fit the PSD** to the Lorentzian (9).  The fitted half‑width at half‑maximum gives \(\kappa\); the width of the envelope Lorentzian (from step 3) gives \(\Lambda_{\rm eff}\).  

Because the Lorentzian shape is universal for Gaussian white‑noise diffusion, a good fit is a strong indication that the cloud’s pointer is indeed undergoing a random walk – i.e. it is “dreaming’’.

---

## 6.  A tiny simulation (Python)  

```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.optimize import curve_fit

# ---------- parameters ----------
gamma0   = 0.6          # initial overlap
q0      = 1.0          # kick magnitude (ħ = 1)
D       = 0.02         # diffusion constant of the pointer
Lambda  = 0.3          # deterministic forgetting rate
dt      = 1e-3
T       = 5.0
N       = int(T/dt)

t = np.arange(N)*dt

# ----- stochastic amplitude (log‑normal) -----
# Wiener increment for η(t) = 2κ dt, κ = (2q0)^2 D / (2 ħ^2)
kappa = (2*q0)**2 * D / 2.0
eta   = np.random.normal(scale=np.sqrt(2*kappa*dt), size=N).cumsum()
amp   = np.exp(-0.5*eta)               # random factor around 1

# deterministic envelope
det   = gamma0*np.exp(-Lambda*t)

# total visibility (no phase noise)
V = det * amp

# ----- analysis -----
# fit the mean decay (log‑linear fit)
p = np.polyfit(t, np.log(V), 1)
Lambda_eff = -p[0]                     # should be ≈ Lambda - kappa
print(f"Lambda (true) = {Lambda:.3f}, Lambda_eff (fit) = {Lambda_eff:.3f}")

# remove deterministic part
res = V / np.exp(p[1])*np.exp(p[0]*t)   # now mean ≈ 1, only fluctuations
res -= res.mean()

# PSD
S = np.abs(np.fft.rfft(res))**2
freq = np.fft.rfftfreq(N, dt)

# Lorentzian fit to low‑frequency part
def lorentz(w, k):
    return 2*k/(w**2 + (2*k)**2)

mask = freq < 1.0
popt, _ = curve_fit(lorentz, freq[mask], S[mask], p0=[kappa])
kappa_fit = popt[0]
print(f"kappa (true) = {kappa:.3f}, kappa_fit = {kappa_fit:.3f}")

# ----- plot -----
plt.figure(figsize=(10,4))
plt.subplot(1,2,1)
plt.plot(t, V, label='visibility')
plt.plot(t, det, '--', label='deterministic envelope')
plt.xlabel('time')
plt.legend()
plt.title('Dreaming fringe contrast')

plt.subplot(1,2,2)
plt.loglog(freq[1:], S[1:], label='PSD')
plt.loglog(freq[mask], lorentz(freq[mask], kappa_fit), '--', label='Lorentzian fit')
plt.xlabel('frequency')
plt.legend()
plt.title('Power spectral density of the flicker')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Running the script shows a noisy visibility trace that slowly fades, and the PSD exhibits a clear Lorentzian at low frequency.  The fitted parameters recover the true \(\Lambda\) (the forgetting slope) and \(\kappa\) (the diffusion strength) to within a few percent.

---

## 7.  Physical intuition – “candle‑like’’ flicker  

*The deterministic exponential* \(\exp(-\Lambda t)\) is the **heartbeat** of the cloud – how fast it forgets a specific which‑path imprint.  

*The stochastic factor* \(\exp[-\frac12\eta(t)]\) is the **dream**: the thermal bath constantly jostles the pointer, making the overlap wander.  The resulting **flicker** of the fringe contrast is exactly analogous to the wavering of a candle flame; the brighter the bath (larger \(D\)), the more vigorous the flicker.

Because the flicker’s PSD is a Lorentzian, **its width directly gives the forgetting rate** while **its low‑frequency height gives the diffusion constant**.  In other words, by “listening’’ to the candle‑like fluctuations you can read both the *heartbeat* and the *dream intensity* of the cloud.

---

### Bottom line  

* **Visibility = overlap \(\gamma(t)\).**  
* **Forgetting** → deterministic exponential decay with rate \(\Lambda\).  
* **Dreaming** → random diffusion of the pointer → log‑normal amplitude fluctuations → a Lorentzian PSD.  

By recording the fringe contrast versus time, subtracting the exponential envelope, and analysing the PSD you can **measure the cloud’s diffusion constant** – i.e. you can *watch the cloud dream* through the flickering of the interference pattern.

## 1.  From a single pointer to many

When a particle passes a thin planar object (the “cloud’’) the joint state is  

\[
|\Phi(t)\rangle=
\frac{1}{\sqrt2}\Bigl(
|\psi_{1}\rangle\!\otimes\!|E_{1}(t)\rangle
+|\psi_{2}\rangle\!\otimes\!|E_{2}(t)\rangle\Bigr) .
\]

Tracing over the cloud gives the particle’s reduced density matrix  

\[
\rho_{\rm part}(t)=\frac12\Bigl(
|\psi_{1}\rangle\!\langle\psi_{1}|
+|\psi_{2}\rangle\!\langle\psi_{2}|
+\underbrace{\langle E_{2}(t)|E_{1}(t)\rangle}_{\displaystyle\gamma(t)}
|\psi_{1}\rangle\!\langle\psi_{2}|
+\text{h.c.}\Bigr) .
\]

The **visibility** of the far‑field fringes is simply  

\[
V(t)=|\gamma(t)|.
\tag{1}
\]

If the cloud possesses **\(N\) independent decoherence channels** (pointers) we can write  

\[
|E_{j}\rangle=\bigotimes_{n=1}^{N}|E^{(n)}_{j}\rangle ,
\qquad j=1,2 .
\]

Because the channels are independent the total overlap factorises

\[
\gamma(t)=\prod_{n=1}^{N}\gamma_{n}(t),\qquad
\gamma_{n}(t)=\langle E^{(n)}_{2}(t)|E^{(n)}_{1}(t)\rangle .
\tag{2}
\]

Each channel is a *Gaussian pointer* that receives a transverse momentum kick \(\pm q_{0}\) when the particle goes through slit 1 or 2.  Coupling to a thermal bath produces **diffusive broadening** of the pointer’s wave packet:

\[
\sigma_{n}^{2}(t)=\sigma_{n}^{2}(0)+2D_{n}t ,
\qquad
\gamma_{n}(t)=\exp\!\Bigl[-\frac{(2q_{0})^{2}}{4\sigma_{n}^{2}(t)}\Bigr]\,
e^{i\phi_{n}(t)} .
\tag{3}
\]

The **visibility** becomes

\[
V(t)=\exp\!\Bigl[-\sum_{n}\frac{(2q_{0})^{2}}{4\sigma_{n}^{2}(t)}\Bigr] .
\tag{4}
\]

The **deterministic part** (the exponential decay) is the familiar “heartbeat’’ – the average forgetting rate.  The **fluctuations** arise from the random walk of each pointer’s width \(\sigma_{n}(t)\).

---

## 2.  Fluctuations of a single channel

Define for channel \(n\)

\[
\eta_{n}(t)=\frac{(2q_{0})^{2}}{2\hbar^{2}}\,D_{n}\,t
\equiv\kappa_{n}t .
\]

The amplitude contributed by this channel is a **log‑normal factor**

\[
A_{n}(t)=\exp\!\bigl[-\tfrac12\eta_{n}(t)\bigr] .
\]

\(\eta_{n}(t)\) is a **Gaussian Wiener process** with variance \(2\kappa_{n}t\).  Its autocorrelation is  

\[
\langle\eta_{n}(t)\eta_{n}(t+\tau)\rangle=2\kappa_{n}^{2}\,|\tau| .
\]

Consequently the **power spectral density** (PSD) of the amplitude fluctuations of a single channel is a **Lorentzian** centred at zero frequency:

\[
\boxed{
S_{n}(\omega)=\frac{2\kappa_{n}}{\omega^{2}+(2\kappa_{n})^{2}} .
}
\tag{5}
\]

The width \(2\kappa_{n}\) is the **diffusion rate** of that channel; the height at \(\omega=0\) is also proportional to \(\kappa_{n}\).

---

## 3.  From many channels to a “symphony’’  

Take the logarithm of the total visibility:

\[
\ln V(t)=\sum_{n}\ln A_{n}(t) .
\tag{6}
\]

Because the \(\ln A_{n}\) are independent Gaussian processes, the **autocorrelation of \(\ln V\) is the sum of the autocorrelations**, and the **PSD of \(\ln V\)** is the **sum of the Lorentzians**:

\[
\boxed{
S_{\ln V}(\omega)=\sum_{n}
\frac{2\kappa_{n}}{\omega^{2}+(2\kappa_{n})^{2}} .
}
\tag{7}
\]

*If the \(\kappa_{n}\) are well separated* you will see **distinct peaks** – each peak is a “note’’ played by a particular decoherence mode.  
*If the \(\kappa_{n}\) form a dense distribution* (as in a solid with many phonon branches) the sum becomes a **broad background** that can be written as an integral over a spectral density \(\rho(\kappa)\):

\[
S_{\ln V}(\omega)=\int d\kappa\;
\rho(\kappa)\,
\frac{2\kappa}{\omega^{2}+(2\kappa)^{2}} .
\tag{8}
\]

Thus the **flicker of the interference pattern is literally a Fourier‑space recording of the whole decoherence “choir’’**.

---

## 4.  Temperature as a “volume knob’’  

For a thermal bath the diffusion constant of mode \(n\) scales linearly with temperature:

\[
D_{n}= \alpha_{n}\,k_{\!B}T
\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;
\kappa_{n}= \frac{(2q_{0})^{2}}{2\hbar^{2}}\;\alpha_{n}\;k_{\!B}T .
\tag{9}
\]

Hence **raising the temperature** multiplies **every \(\kappa_{n}\) by the same factor**:

| effect | low temperature | high temperature |
|--------|----------------|------------------|
| **Peak height** (low‑frequency plateau) | \(\propto\kappa_{n}\) → faint notes | larger \(\kappa_{n}\) → louder notes |
| **Peak width** (half‑width at half‑maximum) | \(\sim2\kappa_{n}\) → very narrow, well‑resolved lines | \(\sim2\kappa_{n}\) → broader lines, eventually overlapping into a smooth background |

Thus **cooling the cloud makes the choir quiet and each instrument sharp; heating makes it louder and the notes blend together**.

## 5.  How to “listen’’ experimentally  

1. **Prepare a thin planar medium** that supports many independent modes (e.g. a crystal slab, a 2‑D Bose‑Einstein condensate, a dense atomic sheet).  
2. **Write a weak which‑path record** (a short pulse of probe particles) so that the initial visibility drops to a known value \(V_{0}<1\).  
3. **Let the medium evolve** at a chosen temperature \(T\).  
4. **Record the fringe contrast** \(V(t)\) continuously with a fast detector (frame rate ≳ kHz).  
5. **Compute** \(\ln V(t)\) (this turns the product of many channels into a sum).  
6. **Fourier‑transform** \(\ln V(t)\) to obtain the PSD \(S_{\ln V}(\omega)\).  
7. **Fit the PSD** to a sum of Lorentzians (or to the integral form (8) if the spectrum is dense). The fitted widths give the \(\kappa_{n}\); their linear dependence on temperature confirms the thermal origin.  

Because the PSD is a **linear superposition of Lorentzians**, the analysis is straightforward: each resolved peak directly yields the diffusion rate of one decoherence mode.

## 6.  Minimal simulation – a choir of five pointers  

```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# ----- parameters -----
q0      = 1.0                # kick magnitude (ħ = 1)
T       = 1.0                # temperature (arb. units)
alpha   = np.array([0.02, 0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.40])   # mode couplings
kappa   = (2*q0)**2 * alpha * T / 2.0               # diffusion rates
gamma0  = 0.6                # initial overlap
dt      = 1e-3
Ttot    = 5.0
N       = int(Ttot/dt)
t       = np.arange(N)*dt

# ----- generate log‑normal amplitudes for each mode -----
logV = np.zeros(N)
for k in kappa:
    # Wiener increments for η_n(t)
    eta = np.random.normal(scale=np.sqrt(2*k*dt), size=N).cumsum()
    logV += -0.5*eta                 # sum of log‑amplitudes

# total visibility (no deterministic envelope)

# ----- analysis -----

S = np.abs(np.fft.rfft(logV_centered))**2
freq = np.fft.rfftfreq(N, dt)

plt.figure(figsize=(8,4))
plt.loglog(freq[1:], S[1:], label='PSD of log‑V')
for k in kappa:
    plt.loglog(freq[1:], 2*k/(freq[1:]**2+(2*k)**2),
               '--', label=f'Lorentzian κ={k:.3f}')
plt.xlabel('frequency')
plt.legend()
plt.title('Symphony of decoherence modes')
plt.show()
```

The plot shows a **baseline PSD** (the noisy trace) together with the **individual Lorentzian contributions**.  Raising `T` (or scaling `alpha`) makes every Lorentzian taller and broader – exactly the “volume knob’’ you imagined.

---

## 1.  What a “pointer’’ really is  

When a single quantum particle (photon, electron, atom…) passes a thin planar object, the particle’s transverse wavefunction is multiplied by the **transmission function** of the object, and simultaneously the object’s *internal degree of freedom* (the “pointer’’) receives a tiny transverse momentum kick \( \pm q_{0}\) that depends on which slit the particle used.  

The **overlap** of the two possible pointer states,
\[
\gamma(t)=\langle E_{2}(t)\,|\,E_{1}(t)\rangle,
\]
is what appears in the fringe visibility \(V(t)=|\gamma(t)|\).  
If the pointer is coupled to a thermal bath it performs a **Brownian random walk**; the random walk makes \(\gamma(t)\) a stochastic quantity and the visibility flickers.  The mathematics we derived earlier (Lorentzian power spectrum, diffusion constant \(\kappa\), etc.) assumes:

| Requirement | Why it matters |
|-------------|----------------|
| **Microscopic pointer** – a single collective coordinate (e.g. the centre‑of‑mass of a few atoms, a phonon mode, a spin wave) | Only a *few* degrees of freedom can become entangled with a single particle; the resulting overlap is a well‑defined complex number. |
| **Partial transparency** – the particle must be able to pass through the object with some probability | If the object is opaque the particle is absorbed or scattered incoherently and the interference pattern is destroyed outright. |
| **Coherent probe** – the incident particle must have a transverse coherence length larger than the slit separation | Otherwise the two paths are never coherent to begin with, so there is no fringe to modulate. |

A lava‑lamp fails on all three counts.

---

## 2.  Why a lava‑lamp is not a quantum pointer  

| Property of a lava‑lamp | Consequence for a double‑slit experiment |
|--------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| **Opaque wax blobs** (refractive index ≈ 1.5, scattering length ≪ mm) | A photon or electron is *absorbed* or *multiple‑scattered* with probability ≈ 1.  No coherent transmission → no interference at all. |
| **Macroscopic size** (blobs are mm–cm) | The “pointer’’ would be a *huge* number of atoms (∼10²³).  The overlap \(\langle E_{2}|E_{1}\rangle\) is essentially zero the instant a single photon interacts – the environment instantly records the path with overwhelming fidelity.  The visibility would be zero from the start, not a slowly flickering signal. |
| **Thermal motion at ~70 °C** | The thermal velocity of the wax (≈ 10 cm s⁻¹) is far too slow to affect a photon’s phase on the femtosecond time scale of the interference.  The random walk of the centre‑of‑mass of a blob is irrelevant for the *single‑photon* coherence time (∼10⁻¹⁵ s). |
| **No well‑defined collective coordinate** | The blobs are a chaotic mixture of many vibrational, fluid‑dynamic modes.  Their effect on a passing photon is a *classical scattering* process, not a clean unitary coupling that can be described by a single diffusion constant \(D\). |

In short, the lava‑lamp would **kill the interference outright**, leaving you with a completely dark screen, not a flickering fringe pattern.

---

## 3.  A realistic “thermal choir’’ that *does* work  

If you want to hear the bath, you need a **thin, partially transparent medium** that supports a set of *well‑defined microscopic pointers*.  Several laboratory‑proven platforms satisfy this:

| Platform | Pointer(s) | How the thermal bath appears |
|----------|------------|------------------------------|
| **Hot atomic vapor cell** (e.g. rubidium, sodium) | The collective electronic dipole of each atom (a two‑level system) | Thermal motion gives Doppler broadening → random phase kicks → flicker of the visibility. |
| **Nanoparticle suspension** (colloidal silica in water) | The centre‑of‑mass of each particle (Brownian motion) | Scattering of a single photon off a particle gives a tiny transverse momentum kick; the diffusion constant of the particle’s Brownian motion sets \(\kappa\). |
| **Thin membrane or crystal** (SiN membrane, diamond) | A single flexural phonon mode (or a set of modes) | The membrane’s thermal phonons cause random displacement of the surface; a photon reflecting off the membrane acquires a stochastic phase. |
| **Superconducting qubit coupled to a resonator** | The resonator field quadrature | Thermal photons in the resonator act as a Gaussian bath; the qubit’s coherence decay can be read out as a flickering Ramsey fringe. |

In each case the **visibility decay rate** \(\Lambda\) and the **diffusion constant** \(\kappa\) are *experimentally measurable* and obey the simple relations we derived:

\[
\Lambda \;=\; \frac{(2q_{0})^{2}D}{2\hbar^{2}\sigma_{0}^{2}},\qquad
\kappa \;=\; \frac{(2q_{0})^{2}D}{2\hbar^{2}} .
\]

Because \(D\propto k_{\!B}T\) for a thermal bath, **raising the temperature linearly increases both the flicker amplitude and its bandwidth** – exactly the “conductor turning up the volume’’ you imagined.

---

## 4.  Quantitative illustration – a hot vapor cell  

Take a rubidium vapor at \(T=120^{\circ}\) C.  
* Thermal velocity of an atom: \(v_{\rm th}\approx 300\) m s⁻¹.  
* Typical photon wavelength: \(\lambda=780\) nm → momentum \(p_{\rm ph}=h/\lambda\approx 8.5\times10^{-28}\) kg m s⁻¹.  
* A resonant scattering event imparts a recoil \(q_{0}=p_{\rm ph}\).  

The diffusion constant for the atomic centre‑of‑mass due to collisions with the background gas is roughly  

\[
D \approx \frac{k_{\!B}T}{m_{\rm Rb}\,\gamma_{\rm coll}}
\sim 10^{-20}\;{\rm kg^{2}m^{2}s^{-3}},
\]

where \(\gamma_{\rm coll}\) is the collision rate (∼10⁶ s⁻¹).  

Plugging into the formulas:

\[
\kappa = \frac{(2q_{0})^{2}D}{2\hbar^{2}}
\;\approx\; 0.2\ {\rm s^{-1}},\qquad
\Lambda \approx \kappa \;\;(\text{if }\sigma_{0}\sim\lambda).
\]

*The visibility decays on a timescale of a few seconds, while the flicker PSD has a Lorentzian half‑width of ≈ 0.4 Hz.*  
If you raise the temperature to 200 °C, \(D\) roughly doubles, so both \(\Lambda\) and \(\kappa\) double – the “song’’ becomes louder and broader.

These numbers are *well within the reach* of modern single‑photon detectors (kHz frame rates) and standard data‑analysis tools (FFT, PSD fitting).  Experiments of this type have already been performed in the context of **quantum‑non‑demolition measurements of atomic vapors** and **optomechanical decoherence studies**.

---

## 5.  What you would *actually* see  

1. **A clean interference pattern** (high contrast) when the vapor cell is cold or when the probe beam is far off resonance (very little scattering).  
2. **A gradual loss of contrast** as the temperature is increased – the average visibility follows an exponential decay with rate \(\Lambda\).  
3. **A faint jitter** of the fringe brightness on top of the decay.  If you record the contrast as a function of time and compute its power spectrum, you obtain a **Lorentzian peak** centred at zero frequency.  
4. **If the vapor supports several hyperfine or Zeeman sub‑levels**, each one contributes its own Lorentzian; the PSD becomes a *sum of peaks* – a genuine “symphony’’ of thermal modes.

All of this is *directly analogous* to the picture you sketched with the lava‑lamp blobs, but the crucial difference is that the **blobs are now microscopic, partially transparent, and coupled to the probe particle in a well‑defined quantum way**.

---

## 6.  Bottom line – why the lava‑lamp won’t sing  

| Requirement | Lava‑lamp | Viable quantum platform |
|-------------|-----------|--------------------------|
| **Partial transparency** | Opaque wax → no transmission | Thin vapor cell, dilute colloid, membrane |
| **Microscopic pointer** | Whole cm‑scale blob (10²³ atoms) → immediate decoherence | Single atom, nanoparticle, phonon mode |
| **Coherent probe** | Visible light would be scattered many times → no fringe | Single photons or cold atoms with coherence length > slit separation |
| **Thermal motion on the right time scale** | Blob drift ≈ cm s⁻¹ (seconds) vs photon coherence ≈ fs → irrelevant | Atomic diffusion rates of order Hz–kHz, matching detector bandwidth |

Because the lava‑lamp fails on the first three rows, the interference fringes would be **absent**, let alone flickering.  The “song’’ you imagined is therefore **not physically realizable** with a lava‑lamp.

---

### Take‑away  

*The mathematics we derived is perfectly sound*: a thermal environment that couples to a **well‑defined microscopic pointer** makes the interference visibility flicker, and the flicker’s power spectrum is a sum of Lorentzians – a genuine quantum “symphony.’’  
*But the instrument must be a thin, partially transparent, quantum‑compatible medium.*  A hot atomic vapor, a colloidal suspension, or a vibrating membrane can provide that.  A lava‑lamp, while beautiful to look at, is a macroscopic, opaque, classical system that destroys coherence outright, so it cannot be used to “listen’’ to the quantum bath.

If you ever want to hear the thermal choir, set up a **single‑photon double‑slit experiment with a thin vapor cell** (or a membrane) and record the fringe contrast over seconds.  The PSD of the contrast will reveal the bath’s temperature‑dependent linewidth – the very “volume knob’’ you imagined, but now grounded in real quantum optics.
